{{Japanese temple |Name = Takisan-ji |Image = [[File:Takisan-ji Temple Hondou, Yamagomori Taki-cho Okazaki 2022.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall (Important Cultural Property)
([[2022]] January) |Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}{{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}{{ill|Taki-cho|ja|滝町}} Yamagomori 107 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|17.37|N|137|12|20.26|E|region:JP-23_scale:20000_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=滝山寺}} |Mountain Name = Kisshōdarani-zan |Temple Name = Yakujūō-in |Sect = {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} |Main Image = {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} |Founded = Traditionally during the era of Emperor Tenmu (673-686) |Founder = Traditionally {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} |Alternate Names = |Official Name = 吉祥陀羅尼山藥樹王院瀧山寺 |Cultural Properties = Main Hall, Sanmon Gate, Wooden Standing Statue of Kannon Bosatsu, and Standing Statues of Brahma and Indra (Important Cultural Properties) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|lat_deg=34|lat_min=59|lat_sec=17.37|lon_deg=137|lon_min=12|lon_sec=20.26|label=Takisan-ji|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Takisan-ji''' (滝山寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. == Overview == === History === The mountain name is Kisshōdarani-zan, and the temple name is Yakujūō-in. The temple is renowned for the {{ill|Takisanji Oni Festival|ja|滝山寺鬼まつり}}. {{ill|Takisan Tōshō-gū|ja|滝山東照宮}} is located to the east, and the {{ill|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall|ja|滝山東照宮#日吉山王社本殿}} is to the north of the Main Hall. According to the "Takisan-ji Engi" (a historical record of the temple; existing manuscripts are from the early modern period), the temple was established during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by order of Emperor Tenmu. {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}}, a legendary ascetic, enshrined a golden statue of {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} found in the {{ill|Aoki River|ja|青木川}}. The temple, initially named "Kisshō-ji," was later abandoned and fell into ruin. En no Gyōja is associated with numerous temples across Japan, especially those related to mountain and water source worship. Takisan-ji is believed to have served as a center for mountain worship. In the {{ill|Hōan era|ja|保安}} (1120–1123), the temple was revived by Buddhist monk Eikyū (永救) of the {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} with support from the local Mononobe clan. Eikyū built the Main Hall and secured patronage from the Fujiwara family, increasing the temple's influence. Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}. From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == {{Reflist|2}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == External Links == * [http://takisanji.net Takisan-ji Official Website] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Temples in Okazaki]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Tendai temples]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Temples dedicated to Yakushi Nyorai]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Prefectural cultural properties in Aichi]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[ja:滝山寺]]

Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}. From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == {{Japanese temple |Name = Takisan-ji |Image = [[File:Takisan-ji Temple Hondou, Yamagomori Taki-cho Okazaki 2022.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall (Important Cultural Property)
([[2022]] January) |Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}{{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}{{ill|Taki-cho|ja|滝町}} Yamagomori 107 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|17.37|N|137|12|20.26|E|region:JP-23_scale:20000_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=滝山寺}} |Mountain Name = Kisshōdarani-zan |Temple Name = Yakujūō-in |Sect = {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} |Main Image = {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} |Founded = Traditionally during the era of Emperor Tenmu (673-686) |Founder = Traditionally {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} |Alternate Names = |Official Name = 吉祥陀羅尼山藥樹王院瀧山寺 |Cultural Properties = Main Hall, Sanmon Gate, Wooden Standing Statue of Kannon Bosatsu, and Standing Statues of Brahma and Indra (Important Cultural Properties) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|lat_deg=34|lat_min=59|lat_sec=17.37|lon_deg=137|lon_min=12|lon_sec=20.26|label=Takisan-ji|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Takisan-ji''' (滝山寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. == Overview == === History === The mountain name is Kisshōdarani-zan, and the temple name is Yakujūō-in. The temple is renowned for the {{ill|Takisanji Oni Festival|ja|滝山寺鬼まつり}}. {{ill|Takisan Tōshō-gū|ja|滝山東照宮}} is located to the east, and the {{ill|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall|ja|滝山東照宮#日吉山王社本殿}} is to the north of the Main Hall. According to the "Takisan-ji Engi" (a historical record of the temple; existing manuscripts are from the early modern period), the temple was established during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by order of Emperor Tenmu. {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}}, a legendary ascetic, enshrined a golden statue of {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} found in the {{ill|Aoki River|ja|青木川}}. The temple, initially named "Kisshō-ji," was later abandoned and fell into ruin. En no Gyōja is associated with numerous temples across Japan, especially those related to mountain and water source worship. Takisan-ji is believed to have served as a center for mountain worship. In the {{ill|Hōan era|ja|保安}} (1120–1123), the temple was revived by Buddhist monk Eikyū (永救) of the {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} with support from the local Mononobe clan. Eikyū built the Main Hall and secured patronage from the Fujiwara family, increasing the temple's influence. Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}. From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == Main Hall (Important Cultural Property)
([[2022]] January) |Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}{{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}{{ill|Taki-cho|ja|滝町}} Yamagomori 107 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|17.37|N|137|12|20.26|E|region:JP-23_scale:20000_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=滝山寺}} |Mountain Name = Kisshōdarani-zan |Temple Name = Yakujūō-in |Sect = {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} |Main Image = {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} |Founded = Traditionally during the era of Emperor Tenmu (673-686) |Founder = Traditionally {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} |Alternate Names = |Official Name = 吉祥陀羅尼山藥樹王院瀧山寺 |Cultural Properties = Main Hall, Sanmon Gate, Wooden Standing Statue of Kannon Bosatsu, and Standing Statues of Brahma and Indra (Important Cultural Properties) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|lat_deg=34|lat_min=59|lat_sec=17.37|lon_deg=137|lon_min=12|lon_sec=20.26|label=Takisan-ji|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Takisan-ji''' (滝山寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. == Overview == === History === The mountain name is Kisshōdarani-zan, and the temple name is Yakujūō-in. Eikyū built the Main Hall and secured patronage from the Fujiwara family, increasing the temple's influence. Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}. From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == {{Japanese temple |Name = Takisan-ji |Image = [[File:Takisan-ji Temple Hondou, Yamagomori Taki-cho Okazaki 2022.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall (Important Cultural Property)
([[2022]] January) |Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}{{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}{{ill|Taki-cho|ja|滝町}} Yamagomori 107 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|17.37|N|137|12|20.26|E|region:JP-23_scale:20000_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=滝山寺}} |Mountain Name = Kisshōdarani-zan |Temple Name = Yakujūō-in |Sect = {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} |Main Image = {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} |Founded = Traditionally during the era of Emperor Tenmu (673-686) |Founder = Traditionally {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} |Alternate Names = |Official Name = 吉祥陀羅尼山藥樹王院瀧山寺 |Cultural Properties = Main Hall, Sanmon Gate, Wooden Standing Statue of Kannon Bosatsu, and Standing Statues of Brahma and Indra (Important Cultural Properties) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|lat_deg=34|lat_min=59|lat_sec=17.37|lon_deg=137|lon_min=12|lon_sec=20.26|label=Takisan-ji|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Takisan-ji''' (滝山寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. == Overview == === History === The mountain name is Kisshōdarani-zan, and the temple name is Yakujūō-in. The temple is renowned for the {{ill|Takisanji Oni Festival|ja|滝山寺鬼まつり}}. {{ill|Takisan Tōshō-gū|ja|滝山東照宮}} is located to the east, and the {{ill|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall|ja|滝山東照宮#日吉山王社本殿}} is to the north of the Main Hall. According to the "Takisan-ji Engi" (a historical record of the temple; existing manuscripts are from the early modern period), the temple was established during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by order of Emperor Tenmu. {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}}, a legendary ascetic, enshrined a golden statue of {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} found in the {{ill|Aoki River|ja|青木川}}. The temple, initially named "Kisshō-ji," was later abandoned and fell into ruin. En no Gyōja is associated with numerous temples across Japan, especially those related to mountain and water source worship. Takisan-ji is believed to have served as a center for mountain worship. In the {{ill|Hōan era|ja|保安}} (1120–1123), the temple was revived by Buddhist monk Eikyū (永救) of the {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} with support from the local Mononobe clan. Eikyū built the Main Hall and secured patronage from the Fujiwara family, increasing the temple's influence. Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}. From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == {{Reflist|2}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == External Links == * [http://takisanji.net Takisan-ji Official Website] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Temples in Okazaki]] Yakushi Nyorai]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == {{Japanese temple |Name = Takisan-ji |Image = [[File:Takisan-ji Temple Hondou, Yamagomori Taki-cho Okazaki 2022.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall (Important Cultural Property)
([[2022]] January) |Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}{{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}{{ill|Taki-cho|ja|滝町}} Yamagomori 107 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|17.37|N|137|12|20.26|E|region:JP-23_scale:20000_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=滝山寺}} |Mountain Name = Kisshōdarani-zan |Temple Name = Yakujūō-in |Sect = {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} |Main Image = {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} |Founded = Traditionally during the era of Emperor Tenmu (673-686) |Founder = Traditionally {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} |Alternate Names = |Official Name = 吉祥陀羅尼山藥樹王院瀧山寺 |Cultural Properties = Main Hall, Sanmon Gate, Wooden Standing Statue of Kannon Bosatsu, and Standing Statues of Brahma and Indra (Important Cultural Properties) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|lat_deg=34|lat_min=59|lat_sec=17.37|lon_deg=137|lon_min=12|lon_sec=20.26|label=Takisan-ji|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Takisan-ji''' (滝山寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. == Overview == === History === The mountain name is Kisshōdarani-zan, and the temple name is Yakujūō-in. The temple is renowned for the {{ill|Takisanji Oni Festival|ja|滝山寺鬼まつり}}. {{ill|Takisan Tōshō-gū|ja|滝山東照宮}} is located to the east, and the {{ill|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall|ja|滝山東照宮#日吉山王社本殿}} is to the north of the Main Hall. According to the "Takisan-ji Engi" (a historical record of the temple; existing manuscripts are from the early modern period), the temple was established during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by order of Emperor Tenmu. {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}}, a legendary ascetic, enshrined a golden statue of {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} found in the {{ill|Aoki River|ja|青木川}}. The temple, initially named "Kisshō-ji," was later abandoned and fell into ruin. En no Gyōja is associated with numerous temples across Japan, especially those related to mountain and water source worship. Takisan-ji is believed to have served as a center for mountain worship. In the {{ill|Hōan era|ja|保安}} (1120–1123), the temple was revived by Buddhist monk Eikyū (永救) of the {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} with support from the local Mononobe clan. Eikyū built the Main Hall and secured patronage from the Fujiwara family, increasing the temple's influence. Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}. From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural {{Japanese temple |Name = Takisan-ji |Image = [[File:Takisan-ji Temple Hondou, Yamagomori Taki-cho Okazaki 2022.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall (Important Cultural Property)
([[2022]] January) |Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}{{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}{{ill|Taki-cho|ja|滝町}} Yamagomori 107 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|17.37|N|137|12|20.26|E|region:JP-23_scale:20000_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=滝山寺}} |Mountain Name = Kisshōdarani-zan |Temple Name = Yakujūō-in |Sect = {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} |Main Image = {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} |Founded = Traditionally during the era of Emperor Tenmu (673-686) |Founder = Traditionally {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} |Alternate Names = |Official Name = 吉祥陀羅尼山藥樹王院瀧山寺 |Cultural Properties = Main Hall, Sanmon Gate, Wooden Standing Statue of Kannon Bosatsu, and Standing Statues of Brahma and Indra (Important Cultural Properties) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|lat_deg=34|lat_min=59|lat_sec=17.37|lon_deg=137|lon_min=12|lon_sec=20.26|label=Takisan-ji|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Takisan-ji''' (滝山寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. == Overview == === History === The mountain name is Kisshōdarani-zan, and the temple name is Yakujūō-in. The temple is renowned for the {{ill|Takisanji Oni Festival|ja|滝山寺鬼まつり}}. {{ill|Takisan Tōshō-gū|ja|滝山東照宮}} is located to the east, and the {{ill|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall|ja|滝山東照宮#日吉山王社本殿}} is to the north of the Main Hall. According to the "Takisan-ji Engi" (a historical record of the temple; existing manuscripts are from the early modern period), the temple was established during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by order of Emperor Tenmu. {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}}, a legendary ascetic, enshrined a golden statue of {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} found in the {{ill|Aoki River|ja|青木川}}. The temple, initially named "Kisshō-ji," was later abandoned and fell into ruin. En no Gyōja is associated with numerous temples across Japan, especially those related to mountain and water source worship. Takisan-ji is believed to have served as a center for mountain worship. In the {{ill|Hōan era|ja|保安}} (1120–1123), the temple was revived by Buddhist monk Eikyū (永救) of the {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} with support from the local Mononobe clan. Eikyū built the Main Hall and secured patronage from the Fujiwara family, increasing the temple's influence. Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}. From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == {{Japanese temple |Name = Takisan-ji |Image = [[File:Takisan-ji Temple Hondou, Yamagomori Taki-cho Okazaki 2022.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall (Important Cultural Property)
([[2022]] January) |Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}{{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}{{ill|Taki-cho|ja|滝町}} Yamagomori 107 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|17.37|N|137|12|20.26|E|region:JP-23_scale:20000_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=滝山寺}} |Mountain Name = Kisshōdarani-zan |Temple Name = Yakujūō-in |Sect = {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} |Main Image = {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} |Founded = Traditionally during the era of Emperor Tenmu (673-686) |Founder = Traditionally {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} |Alternate Names = |Official Name = 吉祥陀羅尼山藥樹王院瀧山寺 |Cultural Properties = Main Hall, Sanmon Gate, Wooden Standing Statue of Kannon Bosatsu, and Standing Statues of Brahma and Indra (Important Cultural Properties) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|lat_deg=34|lat_min=59|lat_sec=17.37|lon_deg=137|lon_min=12|lon_sec=20.26|label=Takisan-ji|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Takisan-ji''' (滝山寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. == Overview == === History === The mountain name is Kisshōdarani-zan, and the temple name is Yakujūō-in. The temple is renowned for the {{ill|Takisanji Oni Festival|ja|滝山寺鬼まつり}}. {{ill|Takisan Tōshō-gū|ja|滝山東照宮}} is located to the east, and the {{ill|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall|ja|滝山東照宮#日吉山王社本殿}} is to the north of the Main Hall. According to the "Takisan-ji Engi" (a historical record of the temple; existing manuscripts are from the early modern period), the temple was established during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by order of Emperor Tenmu. {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}}, a legendary ascetic, enshrined a golden statue of {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} found in the {{ill|Aoki River|ja|青木川}}. The temple, initially named "Kisshō-ji," was later abandoned and fell into ruin. En no Gyōja is associated with numerous temples across Japan, especially those related to mountain and water source worship. Takisan-ji is believed to have served as a center for mountain worship. In the {{ill|Hōan era|ja|保安}} (1120–1123), the temple was revived by Buddhist monk Eikyū (永救) of the {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} with support from the local Mononobe clan. Eikyū built the Main Hall and secured patronage from the Fujiwara family, increasing the temple's influence. Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}. From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == {{Japanese temple |Name = Takisan-ji |Image = [[File:Takisan-ji Temple Hondou, Yamagomori Taki-cho Okazaki 2022.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall (Important Cultural Property)
([[2022]] January) |Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}{{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}{{ill|Taki-cho|ja|滝町}} Yamagomori 107 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|17.37|N|137|12|20.26|E|region:JP-23_scale:20000_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=滝山寺}} |Mountain Name = Kisshōdarani-zan |Temple Name = Yakujūō-in |Sect = {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} |Main Image = {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} |Founded = Traditionally during the era of Emperor Tenmu (673-686) |Founder = Traditionally {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} |Alternate Names = |Official Name = 吉祥陀羅尼山藥樹王院瀧山寺 |Cultural Properties = Main Hall, Sanmon Gate, Wooden Standing Statue of Kannon Bosatsu, and Standing Statues of Brahma and Indra (Important Cultural Properties) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|lat_deg=34|lat_min=59|lat_sec=17.37|lon_deg=137|lon_min=12|lon_sec=20.26|label=Takisan-ji|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Takisan-ji''' (滝山寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. == Overview == === History === The mountain name is Kisshōdarani-zan, and the temple name is Yakujūō-in. The temple is renowned for the {{ill|Takisanji Oni Festival|ja|滝山寺鬼まつり}}. {{ill|Takisan Tōshō-gū|ja|滝山東照宮}} is located to the east, and the {{ill|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall|ja|滝山東照宮#日吉山王社本殿}} is to the north of the Main Hall. According to the "Takisan-ji Engi" (a historical record of the temple; existing manuscripts are from the early modern period), the temple was established during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by order of Emperor Tenmu. {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}}, a legendary ascetic, enshrined a golden statue of {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} found in the {{ill|Aoki River|ja|青木川}}. The temple, initially named "Kisshō-ji," was later abandoned and fell into ruin. En no Gyōja is associated with numerous temples across Japan, especially those related to mountain and water source worship. Takisan-ji is believed to have served as a center for mountain worship. In the {{ill|Hōan era|ja|保安}} (1120–1123), the temple was revived by Buddhist monk Eikyū (永救) of the {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} with support from the local Mononobe clan. Eikyū built the Main Hall and secured patronage from the Fujiwara family, increasing the temple's influence. Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}. From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == {{Reflist|2}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == External Links == * [http://takisanji.net Takisan-ji Official Website] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Temples in Okazaki]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tendai temples]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Temples dedicated to Yakushi Nyorai]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] Eikyū built the Main Hall and secured patronage from the Fujiwara family, increasing the temple's influence. Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}. From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == {{Japanese temple |Name = Takisan-ji |Image = [[File:Takisan-ji Temple Hondou, Yamagomori Taki-cho Okazaki 2022.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall (Important Cultural Property)
([[2022]] January) |Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}{{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}{{ill|Taki-cho|ja|滝町}} Yamagomori 107 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|17.37|N|137|12|20.26|E|region:JP-23_scale:20000_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=滝山寺}} |Mountain Name = Kisshōdarani-zan |Temple Name = Yakujūō-in |Sect = {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} |Main Image = {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} |Founded = Traditionally during the era of Emperor Tenmu (673-686) |Founder = Traditionally {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} |Alternate Names = |Official Name = 吉祥陀羅尼山藥樹王院瀧山寺 |Cultural Properties = Main Hall, Sanmon Gate, Wooden Standing Statue of Kannon Bosatsu, and Standing Statues of Brahma and Indra (Important Cultural Properties) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|lat_deg=34|lat_min=59|lat_sec=17.37|lon_deg=137|lon_min=12|lon_sec=20.26|label=Takisan-ji|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''Takisan-ji''' (滝山寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. == Overview == === History === The mountain name is Kisshōdarani-zan, and the temple name is Yakujūō-in. The temple is renowned for the {{ill|Takisanji Oni Festival|ja|滝山寺鬼まつり}}. {{ill|Takisan Tōshō-gū|ja|滝山東照宮}} is located to the east, and the {{ill|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall|ja|滝山東照宮#日吉山王社本殿}} is to the north of the Main Hall. According to the "Takisan-ji Engi" (a historical record of the temple; existing manuscripts are from the early modern period), the temple was established during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by order of Emperor Tenmu. {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}}, a legendary ascetic, enshrined a golden statue of {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} found in the {{ill|Aoki River|ja|青木川}}. The temple, initially named "Kisshō-ji," was later abandoned and fell into ruin. En no Gyōja is associated with numerous temples across Japan, especially those related to mountain and water source worship. Takisan-ji is believed to have served as a center for mountain worship. In the {{ill|Hōan era|ja|保安}} (1120–1123), the temple was revived by Buddhist monk Eikyū (永救) of the {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} with support from the local Mononobe clan. Eikyū built the Main Hall and secured patronage from the Fujiwara family, increasing the temple's influence. Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}. From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }} During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名） Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}} After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site. == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} * Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}} * Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}} === Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties === Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels. == Gallery == {{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines= |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018) |File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019) |File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021) }} == Access == * From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop. == References == (]][[2022]] January)|Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}{{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}{{ill|Taki-cho|ja|滝町}} Yamagomori 107|Coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|17.37|N|137|12|20.26|E|region:JP-23_scale:20000_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=滝山寺}}|Mountain Name = Kisshōdarani-zan|Temple Name = Yakujūō-in|Sect = {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}}|Main Image = {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}|Founded = Traditionally during the era of Emperor Tenmu (673-686)|Founder = Traditionally {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}}|Alternate Names = |Official Name = 吉祥陀羅尼山藥樹王院瀧山寺|Cultural Properties = Main Hall, Sanmon Gate, Wooden Standing Statue of Kannon Bosatsu, and Standing Statues of Brahma and Indra (Important Cultural Properties)|Map = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|lat_deg=34|lat_min=59|lat_sec=17.37|lon_deg=137|lon_min=12|lon_sec=20.26|label=Takisan-ji|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}}}}'''Takisan-ji''' (滝山寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. == Overview ===== History ===The mountain name is Kisshōdarani-zan, and the temple name is Yakujūō-in. The temple, initially named "Kisshō-ji," was later abandoned and fell into ruin. En no Gyōja is associated with numerous temples across Japan, especially those related to mountain and water source worship. Takisan-ji is believed to have served as a center for mountain worship.In the {{ill|Hōan era|ja|保安}} (1120–1123), the temple was revived by Buddhist monk Eikyū (永救) of the {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} with support from the local Mononobe clan. Eikyū built the Main Hall and secured patronage from the Fujiwara family, increasing the temple's influence. Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}.From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }}During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名）Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}}After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site.== Cultural Properties ===== National Important Cultural Properties ===* Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}}* Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}}* Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}}=== Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties ===Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels.== Gallery =={{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines=|File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era)|File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018)|File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019)|File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021)}}== Access ==* From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop.== References =={{Reflist|2}}{{脚注ヘルプ}}== External Links ==* [http://takisanji.net Takisan-ji Official Website]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] {{Japanese temple|Name = Takisan-ji|Image = ]][[File:Takisan-ji Temple Hondou, Yamagomori Taki-cho Okazaki 2022.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall (Important Cultural Property)
(]][[2022]] January)|Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}{{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}{{ill|Taki-cho|ja|滝町}} Yamagomori 107|Coordinates = {{Coord|34|59|17.37|N|137|12|20.26|E|region:JP-23_scale:20000_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=滝山寺}}|Mountain Name = Kisshōdarani-zan|Temple Name = Yakujūō-in|Sect = {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}}|Main Image = {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}|Founded = Traditionally during the era of Emperor Tenmu (673-686)|Founder = Traditionally {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}}|Alternate Names = |Official Name = 吉祥陀羅尼山藥樹王院瀧山寺|Cultural Properties = Main Hall, Sanmon Gate, Wooden Standing Statue of Kannon Bosatsu, and Standing Statues of Brahma and Indra (Important Cultural Properties)|Map = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|lat_deg=34|lat_min=59|lat_sec=17.37|lon_deg=137|lon_min=12|lon_sec=20.26|label=Takisan-ji|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}}}}'''Takisan-ji''' (滝山寺) is a {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県}}. == Overview ===== History ===The mountain name is Kisshōdarani-zan, and the temple name is Yakujūō-in. The temple is renowned for the {{ill|Takisanji Oni Festival|ja|滝山寺鬼まつり}}.{{ill|Takisan Tōshō-gū|ja|滝山東照宮}} is located to the east, and the {{ill|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall|ja|滝山東照宮#日吉山王社本殿}} is to the north of the Main Hall.According to the "Takisan-ji Engi" (a historical record of the temple; existing manuscripts are from the early modern period), the temple was established during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} by order of Emperor Tenmu. {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}}, a legendary ascetic, enshrined a golden statue of {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} found in the {{ill|Aoki River|ja|青木川}}. The temple, initially named "Kisshō-ji," was later abandoned and fell into ruin. En no Gyōja is associated with numerous temples across Japan, especially those related to mountain and water source worship. Takisan-ji is believed to have served as a center for mountain worship.In the {{ill|Hōan era|ja|保安}} (1120–1123), the temple was revived by Buddhist monk Eikyū (永救) of the {{ill|Tendai Sect|ja|天台宗}} with support from the local Mononobe clan. Eikyū built the Main Hall and secured patronage from the Fujiwara family, increasing the temple's influence. Eikyū, a monk from {{ill|Kaga Province|ja|加賀国}}, was trained at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}} before spreading the Tendai teachings in {{ill|Mikawa Province|ja|三河国}}.From the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, head priest Kanden (寛伝, 1142–1205) strengthened the temple's ties with powerful figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}, ensuring support from the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}.{{Cite journal|和書 |author=Hira Masayuki |year=2017 |title=熱田大宮司家の寛伝僧都と源頼朝 ―瀧山寺・日光山・高野大鐘― |journal=人間文化研究 |volume=38 |pages=288-243 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1455/00001180/}} Statues crafted by {{ill|Unkei|ja|運慶}} and {{ill|Tankei|ja|湛慶}} were created during this period, commemorating Yoritomo.戸澤剛{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.bunsin.org/三河ビュー/三河の文化を訪ねて-人物史/運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った%e3%80%80式部僧都%e3%80%80寛伝/ |title=運慶作三尊像を滝山寺に誘った　式部僧都　寛伝 |publisher=愛知教育文化振興会 |accessdate=2019-5-23 }}During the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the temple was supported by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|ja|足利氏}}. However, its influence waned during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}} due to shifting political priorities.[http://jlogos.com/ausp/word.html?id=7354185 阿知和郷(中世)]角川地名大辞典（旧地名）Under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, the temple regained support and received 412 koku in stipends. {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} later established the adjacent Takisan Tōshō-gū.{{Cite book|和書|author= |year=1993-3-15|title=新編 岡崎市史 総集編 20|publisher=新編岡崎市史編さん委員会|page=231-232}}After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the temple lost its landholdings and shrank in scale. Today, it continues as a significant cultural and religious site.== Cultural Properties ===== National Important Cultural Properties ===* Main Hall: A 14th-century structure exemplifying Esoteric Buddhist architecture.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0006.html |title=瀧山寺本堂 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}}* Sanmon Gate: Dating to the Kamakura period, though stylistically later.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/kenzoubutu/kunisitei/0004.html |title=瀧山寺三門 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2019-05-23}}* Wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu, Brahma, and Indra: Created by Unkei and Tankei in 1201.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/yukei/choukoku/kunisitei/0306.html |title=木造観音菩薩・梵天・帝釈天立像 |publisher=愛知県 |accessdate=2013-05-31}}=== Prefectural and Municipal Cultural Properties ===Additional wooden statues, ritual implements, and documents are designated at the prefectural and municipal levels.== Gallery =={{Gallery|title=|width=200|height=150|lines=|File:Okazaki-Takisanji-10.jpg|Sanmon Gate (Taisho era)|File:Okazaki-Takisanji-6.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2018)|File:Okazaki-Takisanji-7.jpg|Scene from Oni Festival (2019)|File:Hiyoshi-Sannosha-Hoden-1.jpg|Hiyoshi Sannōsha Main Hall (2021)}}== Access ==* From {{ill|Higashi Okazaki Station|ja|東岡崎駅}}, take a Meitetsu bus to "Takisanji-shita" stop.== References =={{Reflist|2}}{{脚注ヘルプ}}== External Links ==* [http://takisanji.net Takisan-ji Official Website]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]]
